input.description: Explore chat template handlers in llama.cpp
input.prompt:
I need to understand the chat template handler system in llama.cpp. Please thoroughly explore the codebase and find:

1. The main file(s) that contain chat template handlers/implementations (look for things like "apply_chat_template", "llama_chat_template", template handlers for formats like "mistral", "llama", etc.)
2. The header file(s) that declare these functions
3. How templates are registered/auto-detected
4. The existing "ministral-3" or similar Mistral-related handler specifically - find its implementation
5. The pattern/structure used for all template handlers

Key things to search for:
- "ministral" or "mistral" in source files
- "apply_chat_template" function
- "[INST]" and "[/INST]" markers
- "[SYSTEM_PROMPT]" marker
- How template auto-detection works (look for if/else chains or maps that match template names)

Please search very thoroughly - look in src/, common/, include/ directories. Show me the actual code for the ministral/mistral handlers and the auto-detection mechanism.

1	# Instructions for llama.cpp
2	
3	> [!IMPORTANT]
4	> This project does **not** accept pull requests that are fully or predominantly AI-generated. AI tools may be utilized solely in an assistive capacity.
5	>
6	> Read more: [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)
7	
8	AI assistance is permissible only when the majority of the code is authored by a human contributor, with AI employed exclusively for corrections or to expand on verbose modifications that the contributor has already conceptualized (see examples below).
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Guidelines for Contributors Using AI
13	
14	llama.cpp is built by humans, for humans. Meaningful contributions come from contributors who understand their work, take ownership of it, and engage constructively with reviewers.
15	
16	Maintainers receive numerous pull requests weekly, many of which are AI-generated submissions where the author cannot adequately explain the code, debug issues, or participate in substantive design discussions. Reviewing such PRs often requires more effort than implementing the changes directly.
17	
18	**A pull request represents a long-term commitment.** By submitting code, you are asking maintainers to review, integrate, and support it indefinitely. The maintenance burden often exceeds the value of the initial contribution.
19	
20	Most maintainers already have access to AI tools. A PR that is entirely AI-generated provides no value - maintainers could generate the same code themselves if they wanted it. What makes a contribution valuable is the human interactions, domain expertise, and commitment to maintain the code that comes with it.
21	
22	This policy exists to ensure that maintainers can sustainably manage the project without being overwhelmed by low-quality submissions.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Guidelines for Contributors
27	
28	Contributors are expected to:
29	
30	1. **Demonstrate full understanding of their code.** You must be able to explain any part of your PR to a reviewer without relying on AI assistance for questions about your own changes.
31	
32	2. **Take responsibility for maintenance.** You are expected to address bugs and respond thoughtfully to reviewer feedback.
33	
34	3. **Communicate clearly and concisely.** Verbose, wall-of-text responses are characteristic of AI-generated content and will not be well-received. Direct, human communication is expected.
35	
36	4. **Respect maintainers' time.** Search for existing issues and discussions before submitting. Ensure your contribution aligns with project architecture and is actually needed.
37	
38	Maintainers reserve the right to close any PR that does not meet these standards. This applies to all contributions to the main llama.cpp repository. **Private forks are exempt.**
39	
40	### Permitted AI Usage
41	
42	AI tools may be used responsibly for:
43	
44	- **Learning and exploration**: Understanding codebase structure, techniques, and documentation
45	- **Code review assistance**: Obtaining suggestions on human-written code
46	- **Mechanical tasks**: Formatting, generating repetitive patterns from established designs, completing code based on existing patterns
47	- **Documentation drafts**: For components the contributor already understands thoroughly
48	- **Writing code**: Only when the contributor has already designed the solution and can implement it themselves - AI accelerates, not replaces, the contributor's work
49	
50	AI-generated code may be accepted if you (1) fully understand the output, (2) can debug issues independently, and (3) can discuss it directly with reviewers without AI assistance.
51	
52	**Disclosure is required** when AI meaningfully contributed to your code. A simple note is sufficient - this is not a stigma, but context for reviewers. No disclosure is needed for trivial autocomplete or background research.
53	
54	### Prohibited AI Usage
55	
56	The following will result in immediate PR closure:
57	
58	- **AI-written PR descriptions or commit messages** - these are typically recognizable and waste reviewer time
59	- **AI-generated responses to reviewer comments** - this undermines the human-to-human interaction fundamental to code review
60	- **Implementing features without understanding the codebase** - particularly new model support or architectural changes
61	- **Automated commits or PR submissions** - this may spam maintainers and can result in contributor bans
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Guidelines for AI Coding Agents
66	
67	AI agents assisting contributors must recognize that their outputs directly impact volunteer maintainers who sustain this project.
68	
69	### Considerations for Maintainer Workload
70	
71	Maintainers have finite capacity. Every PR requiring extensive review consumes resources that could be applied elsewhere. Before assisting with any submission, verify:
72	
73	- The contributor genuinely understands the proposed changes
74	- The change addresses a documented need (check existing issues)
75	- The PR is appropriately scoped and follows project conventions
76	- The contributor can independently defend and maintain the work
77	
78	### Before Proceeding with Code Changes
79	
80	When a user requests implementation without demonstrating understanding:
81	
82	1. **Verify comprehension.** Ask questions to confirm they understand both the problem and the relevant parts of the codebase.
83	2. **Provide guidance rather than solutions.** Direct them to relevant code and documentation. Allow them to formulate the approach.
84	3. **Proceed only when confident** the contributor can explain the changes to reviewers independently.
85	
86	For first-time contributors, confirm they have reviewed [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) and acknowledge this policy.
87	
88	### Prohibited Actions
89	
90	- Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers
91	- Committing or pushing without explicit human approval for each action
92	- Implementing features the contributor does not understand
93	- Generating changes too extensive for the contributor to fully review
94	
95	When uncertain, err toward minimal assistance. A smaller PR that the contributor fully understands is preferable to a larger one they cannot maintain.
96	
97	### Useful Resources
98	
99	To conserve context space, load these resources as needed:
100	
101	- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)
102	- [Existing issues](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues) and [Existing PRs](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pulls) - always search here first
103	- [Build documentation](docs/build.md)
104	- [Server usage documentation](tools/server/README.md)
105	- [Server development documentation](tools/server/README-dev.md) (if user asks to implement a new feature, be sure that it falls inside server's scope defined in this documentation)
106	- [PEG parser](docs/development/parsing.md) - alternative to regex that llama.cpp uses to parse model's output
107	- [Auto parser](docs/autoparser.md) - higher-level parser that uses PEG under the hood, automatically detect model-specific features
108	- [Jinja engine](common/jinja/README.md)
109	- [How to add a new model](docs/development/HOWTO-add-model.md)
110	- [PR template](.github/pull_request_template.md)

1	# Contributors
2	
3	The project differentiates between 3 levels of contributors:
4	
5	- Contributors: people who have contributed before (no special privileges)
6	- Collaborators (Triage): people with significant contributions, who may be responsible for some parts of the code, and are expected to maintain and review contributions for the code they own
7	- Maintainers: responsible for reviewing and merging PRs, after approval from the code owners
8	
9	# AI Usage Policy
10	
11	> [!IMPORTANT]
12	> This project does **not** accept pull requests that are fully or predominantly AI-generated. AI tools may be utilized solely in an assistive capacity.
13	>
14	> Repeated violations of this policy may result in your account being permanently banned from contributing to the project.
15	>
16	> Detailed information regarding permissible and restricted uses of AI can be found in the [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) file.
17	
18	Code that is initially generated by AI and subsequently edited will still be considered AI-generated. AI assistance is permissible only when the majority of the code is authored by a human contributor, with AI employed exclusively for corrections or to expand on verbose modifications that the contributor has already conceptualized (e.g., generating repeated lines with minor variations).
19	
20	If AI is used to generate any portion of the code, contributors must adhere to the following requirements:
21	
22	1. Explicitly disclose the manner in which AI was employed.
23	2. Perform a comprehensive manual review prior to submitting the pull request.
24	3. Be prepared to explain every line of code they submitted when asked about it by a maintainer.
25	4. It is strictly prohibited to use AI to write your posts for you (bug reports, feature requests, pull request descriptions, Github discussions, responding to humans, ...).
26	
27	For more info, please refer to the [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) file.
28	
29	# Pull requests (for contributors & collaborators)
30	
31	Before submitting your PR:
32	- Search for existing PRs to prevent duplicating efforts
33	- llama.cpp uses the ggml tensor library for model evaluation. If you are unfamiliar with ggml, consider taking a look at the [examples in the ggml repository](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/tree/master/examples/). [simple](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/tree/master/examples/simple) shows the bare minimum for using ggml. [gpt-2](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/tree/master/examples/gpt-2) has minimal implementations for language model inference using GPT-2. [mnist](https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/tree/master/examples/mnist) demonstrates how to train and evaluate a simple image classifier
34	- Test your changes:
35	  - Execute [the full CI locally on your machine](ci/README.md) before publishing
36	  - Verify that the perplexity and the performance are not affected negatively by your changes (use `llama-perplexity` and `llama-bench`)
37	  - If you modified the `ggml` source, run the `test-backend-ops` tool to check whether different backend implementations of the `ggml` operators produce consistent results (this requires access to at least two different `ggml` backends)
38	  - If you modified a `ggml` operator or added a new one, add the corresponding test cases to `test-backend-ops`
39	- Create separate PRs for each feature or fix:
40	  - Avoid combining unrelated changes in a single PR
41	  - For intricate features, consider opening a feature request first to discuss and align expectations
42	  - When adding support for a new model or feature, focus on **CPU support only** in the initial PR unless you have a good reason not to. Add support for other backends like CUDA in follow-up PRs
43	  - In particular, adding new data types (extension of the `ggml_type` enum) carries with it a disproportionate maintenance burden. As such, to add a new quantization type you will need to meet the following *additional* criteria *at minimum*:
44	    - convert a small model to GGUF using the new type and upload it to HuggingFace
45	    - provide [perplexity](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/tree/master/tools/perplexity) comparisons to FP16/BF16 (whichever is the native precision) as well as to types of similar size
46	    - provide KL divergence data calculated vs. the FP16/BF16 (whichever is the native precision) version for both the new type as well as types of similar size
47	    - provide [performance data](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/tree/master/tools/llama-bench) for the new type in comparison to types of similar size on pure CPU
48	- Consider allowing write access to your branch for faster reviews, as reviewers can push commits directly
49	- If you are a new contributor, limit your open PRs to 1.
50	
51	After submitting your PR:
52	- Expect requests for modifications to ensure the code meets llama.cpp's standards for quality and long-term maintainability
53	- Maintainers will rely on your insights and approval when making a final decision to approve and merge a PR
54	- If your PR becomes stale, rebase it on top of latest `master` to get maintainers attention
55	- Consider adding yourself to [CODEOWNERS](CODEOWNERS) to indicate your availability for fixing related issues and reviewing related PRs
56	
57	# Pull requests (for maintainers)
58	
59	- Squash-merge PRs
60	- Use the following format for the squashed commit title: `<module> : <commit title> (#<issue_number>)`. For example: `utils : fix typo in utils.py (#1234)`
61	- Optionally pick a `<module>` from here: https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/wiki/Modules
62	- Let other maintainers merge their own PRs
63	- When merging a PR, make sure you have a good understanding of the changes
64	- Be mindful of maintenance: most of the work going into a feature happens after the PR is merged. If the PR author is not committed to contribute long-term, someone else needs to take responsibility (you)
65	
66	Maintainers reserve the right to decline review or close pull requests for any reason, without any questions, particularly under any of the following conditions:
67	- The proposed change is already mentioned in the roadmap or an existing issue, and it has been assigned to someone.
68	- The pull request duplicates an existing one.
69	- The contributor fails to adhere to this contributing guide or the AI policy.
70	
71	# Coding guidelines
72	
73	- Avoid adding third-party dependencies, extra files, extra headers, etc.
74	- Always consider cross-compatibility with other operating systems and architectures
75	- Avoid fancy-looking modern STL constructs, use basic `for` loops, avoid templates, keep it simple
76	- Vertical alignment makes things more readable and easier to batch edit
77	- Clean-up any trailing whitespaces, use 4 spaces for indentation, brackets on the same line, `void * ptr`, `int & a`
78	- Use sized integer types such as `int32_t` in the public API, e.g. `size_t` may also be appropriate for allocation sizes or byte offsets
79	- Declare structs with `struct foo {}` instead of `typedef struct foo {} foo`
80	    - In C++ code omit optional `struct` and `enum` keyword whenever they are not necessary
81	    ```cpp
82	    // OK
83	    llama_context * ctx;
84	    const llama_rope_type rope_type;
85	
86	    // not OK
87	    struct llama_context * ctx;
88	    const enum llama_rope_type rope_type;
89	    ```
90	
91	    _(NOTE: this guideline is yet to be applied to the `llama.cpp` codebase. New code should follow this guideline.)_
92	
93	- Try to follow the existing patterns in the code (indentation, spaces, etc.). In case of doubt use `clang-format` (from clang-tools v15+) to format the added code
94	- For anything not covered in the current guidelines, refer to the [C++ Core Guidelines](https://isocpp.github.io/CppCoreGuidelines/CppCoreGuidelines)
95	- Tensors store data in row-major order. We refer to dimension 0 as columns, 1 as rows, 2 as matrices
96	- Matrix multiplication is unconventional: [`C = ggml_mul_mat(ctx, A, B)`](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/880e352277fc017df4d5794f0c21c44e1eae2b84/ggml.h#L1058-L1064) means $C^T = A B^T \Leftrightarrow C = B A^T.$
97	
98	![matmul](media/matmul.png)
99	
100	# Naming guidelines
101	
102	- Use `snake_case` for function, variable and type names
103	- Naming usually optimizes for longest common prefix (see https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml/pull/302#discussion_r1243240963)
104	
105	    ```cpp
106	    // not OK
107	    int small_number;
108	    int big_number;
109	
110	    // OK
111	    int number_small;
112	    int number_big;
113	    ```
114	
115	- Enum values are always in upper case and prefixed with the enum name
116	
117	    ```cpp
118	    enum llama_vocab_type {
119	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_NONE = 0,
120	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_SPM  = 1,
121	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_BPE  = 2,
122	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_WPM  = 3,
123	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_UGM  = 4,
124	        LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_RWKV = 5,
125	    };
126	    ```
127	
128	- The general naming pattern is `<class>_<method>`, with `<method>` being `<action>_<noun>`
129	
130	    ```cpp
131	    llama_model_init();           // class: "llama_model",         method: "init"
132	    llama_sampler_chain_remove(); // class: "llama_sampler_chain", method: "remove"
133	    llama_sampler_get_seed();     // class: "llama_sampler",       method: "get_seed"
134	    llama_set_embeddings();       // class: "llama_context",       method: "set_embeddings"
135	    llama_n_threads();            // class: "llama_context",       method: "n_threads"
136	    llama_adapter_lora_free();    // class: "llama_adapter_lora",  method: "free"
137	    ```
138	
139	    - The `get` `<action>` can be omitted
140	    - The `<noun>` can be omitted if not necessary
141	    - The `_context` suffix of the `<class>` is optional. Use it to disambiguate symbols when needed
142	    - Use `init`/`free` for constructor/destructor `<action>`
143	
144	- Use the `_t` suffix when a type is supposed to be opaque to the user - it's not relevant to them if it is a struct or anything else
145	
146	    ```cpp
147	    typedef struct llama_context * llama_context_t;
148	
149	    enum llama_pooling_type llama_pooling_type(const llama_context_t ctx);
150	    ```
151	
152	    _(NOTE: this guideline is yet to be applied to the `llama.cpp` codebase. New code should follow this guideline)_
153	
154	- C/C++ filenames are all lowercase with dashes. Headers use the `.h` extension. Source files use the `.c` or `.cpp` extension
155	- Python filenames are all lowercase with underscores

Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive report of my findings:

## Project Structure and Conventions Report

I've thoroughly reviewed the llama.cpp project structure and key documentation files. Here's everything you need to know before implementing a new chat template handler:

---

## **Top-Level Directory Structure**

The project is organized into these main directories:

```
/                          # Root - configuration and Python utilities
├── src/                   # Main C/C++ source code (llama-*.cpp/h files)
├── common/                # Shared utilities and components
├── docs/                  # Documentation (build, development, features)
├── tools/                 # Executable tools and utilities
│   ├── server/           # LLM server implementation
│   ├── cli/              # Command-line interface tools
│   ├── perplexity/       # Performance measurement
│   ├── llama-bench/      # Benchmarking
│   └── [other tools]     # chat, completion, tokenize, etc.
├── examples/             # Example implementations
├── models/               # Model implementations
├── tests/                # Test suite
├── scripts/              # Build and conversion scripts
├── cmake/                # CMake build configuration
├── ggml/                 # GGML tensor library (dependency)
├── gguf-py/              # Python GGUF utilities
├── grammars/             # GBNF grammar files
├── requirements/         # Dependency specifications
├── benches/              # Benchmark configurations
├── ci/                   # CI/CD configuration
└── vendor/               # Third-party dependencies
```

---

## **AGENTS.md - AI Usage Policy (Critical Read)**

### Key Points:

1. **Strict AI Usage Policy**
   - The project **does not accept fully or predominantly AI-generated PRs**
   - AI assistance is **only permitted in an assistive capacity**
   - Human contributors must author the majority of code
   - Violations may result in permanent account bans

2. **Requirements for AI-Assisted Code**
   - Must demonstrate full understanding of every line of code
   - Must be able to explain code independently to reviewers
   - AI can help with: learning, code review, mechanical tasks, documentation, and accelerating existing designs
   - AI cannot: write PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers

3. **For AI Coding Agents (Important for Your Role)**
   - Verify the contributor genuinely understands proposed changes
   - Check the change addresses a documented need (search existing issues)
   - PR should follow project conventions
   - Contributor must be able to independently defend the work
   - **When uncertain, provide guidance rather than solutions**
   - **Proceed only when confident the contributor understands the changes**

4. **Prohibited Actions**
   - Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or reviewer responses
   - Committing/pushing without explicit human approval
   - Implementing features the contributor doesn't understand
   - Generating changes too extensive for contributor to fully review

5. **Useful Resources Referenced**
   - CONTRIBUTING.md - detailed guidelines
   - Server development documentation: `tools/server/README-dev.md`
   - PEG parser documentation: `docs/development/parsing.md`
   - Auto parser: `docs/autoparser.md`
   - Jinja engine: `common/jinja/README.md`
   - Chat template system: `common/chat*.{cpp,h}`

---

## **CLAUDE.md**

Contains a single important reference: **"Ensure you've thoroughly reviewed the AGENTS.md file before beginning any work."**

This emphasizes that AGENTS.md is the foundational document for all contributions.

---

## **CONTRIBUTING.md - Coding Standards and Guidelines**

### Contributor Levels
- **Contributors**: Previous contributors, no special privileges
- **Collaborators (Triage)**: Significant contributions, code ownership, review responsibilities
- **Maintainers**: Approve and merge PRs

### AI Usage Policy Details
- AI-initially-generated code that is edited is still considered AI-generated
- Requires: explicit disclosure, comprehensive manual review, ability to explain every line
- **Strictly prohibited**: AI-written PR descriptions, bug reports, feature requests, responses to humans

### PR Requirements Before Submission
- Search for existing PRs to avoid duplication
- Familiarize yourself with GGML (tensor library): https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml
- Test changes locally:
  - Run full CI locally (see `ci/README.md`)
  - Verify perplexity/performance using `llama-perplexity` and `llama-bench`
  - If modified GGML: run `test-backend-ops`
- Create **separate PRs for each feature or fix**
- Initial PRs should focus on **CPU support only** (backends in follow-ups)
- **Consider allowing write access** to your branch for faster reviews

### PR Format (for Maintainers)
- Use: `<module> : <commit title> (#<issue_number>)`
- Example: `utils : fix typo in utils.py (#1234)`

### Coding Guidelines
1. **Avoid third-party dependencies, extra files, extra headers**
2. **Cross-compatibility**: Consider all operating systems and architectures
3. **Code style**: 
   - No fancy STL constructs, use basic `for` loops, avoid templates
   - Vertical alignment for readability
   - 4 spaces indentation, brackets on same line
   - Clean up trailing whitespace
4. **Types**: Use sized integer types (`int32_t`, `size_t`) in public APIs
5. **Structs**: Use `struct foo {}` instead of `typedef struct foo {} foo`
6. **Formatting**: Use `clang-format` (v15+) to format code
7. **Tensors**: Store data in row-major order; refer to dimensions as columns (0), rows (1), matrices (2)
8. **Matrix Multiplication**: Unconventional: `C = ggml_mul_mat(ctx, A, B)` means $C^T = A B^T$

### Naming Conventions
1. **Functions/variables/types**: Use `snake_case`
2. **Naming pattern**: `<class>_<method>` where `<method>` is `<action>_<noun>`
   - Examples:
     - `llama_model_init()`
     - `llama_sampler_chain_remove()`
     - `llama_sampler_get_seed()`
3. **Enum values**: UPPERCASE, prefixed with enum name
   ```cpp
   enum llama_vocab_type {
       LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_NONE = 0,
       LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_SPM  = 1,
       // ...
   };
   ```
4. **Filenames**: Lowercase with dashes (C/C++: `.c`/`.cpp`/`.h`), underscores (Python)
5. **Opaque types**: Use `_t` suffix for types meant to be opaque to users

### Code Maintenance
- Large code additions require a collaborator to review and maintain long-term
- Existing code has designated collaborators in `CODEOWNERS`
- New code must follow these guidelines (legacy code exempt for historical reasons)
- Server changes: refer to `tools/server/README-dev.md`

---

## **Project Architecture Insights**

### Common Directory Contents (Chat-Related)
The `common/` directory contains chat template and parser infrastructure:
- `chat.cpp`, `chat.h` - Core chat functionality
- `chat-auto-parser-generator.cpp` - Auto-generates parsers
- `chat-auto-parser-helpers.cpp`, `chat-auto-parser-helpers.h` - Helper utilities
- `chat-auto-parser.h` - Auto parser interface
- `chat-peg-parser.cpp`, `chat-peg-parser.h` - PEG-based parsing
- `chat-diff-analyzer.cpp` - Analyzes differences

### Documentation for Chat Templates
Key documentation files:
- `docs/autoparser.md` - Higher-level parser using PEG under the hood
- `docs/development/parsing.md` - PEG parser documentation
- `common/jinja/README.md` - Jinja template engine

### Tools for Chat Processing
- `tools/server/` - Server with chat endpoint support
- `tools/server/README-dev.md` - Development guidelines for server features
- `tools/tokenize/` - Tokenization tool
- `tools/completion/` - Completion tool

### Server Scope
The server has its own scope and development guidelines. Check `tools/server/README-dev.md` to ensure your chat template handler falls within server's design scope.

---

## **Key Takeaways for Chat Template Handler Implementation**

1. **Start with understanding**: Review existing chat template code in `common/chat*.{cpp,h}` and `docs/autoparser.md`

2. **Follow naming**: Use snake_case for all identifiers, follow `<class>_<method>` pattern

3. **Keep it simple**: 
   - Avoid fancy STL constructs
   - Use basic loops
   - Avoid templates
   - Vertical alignment for readability

4. **Testing before PR**:
   - Verify with `llama-perplexity` and `llama-bench`
   - Run full CI locally
   - Create separate PR for this feature

5. **Architecture**: 
   - The chat system uses PEG parsing or auto-parsing
   - Jinja templating for model-specific chat templates
   - Server integration point

## Project Structure and Conventions Report

I've thoroughly reviewed the llama.cpp project structure and key documentation files. Here's everything you need to know before implementing a new chat template handler:

## **Top-Level Directory Structure**

The project is organized into these main directories:

```
/                          # Root - configuration and Python utilities
├── src/                   # Main C/C++ source code (llama-*.cpp/h files)
├── common/                # Shared utilities and components

│   ├── cli/              # Command-line interface tools

├── examples/             # Example implementations
├── models/               # Model implementations
├── tests/                # Test suite
├── scripts/              # Build and conversion scripts
├── cmake/                # CMake build configuration
├── ggml/                 # GGML tensor library (dependency)
├── gguf-py/              # Python GGUF utilities

├── requirements/         # Dependency specifications
├── benches/              # Benchmark configurations
├── ci/                   # CI/CD configuration
└── vendor/               # Third-party dependencies
```

---

## **AGENTS.md - AI Usage Policy (Critical Read)**

### Key Points:

1. **Strict AI Usage Policy**
   - The project **does not accept fully or predominantly AI-generated PRs**
   - AI assistance is **only permitted in an assistive capacity**
   - Human contributors must author the majority of code
   - Violations may result in permanent account bans

2. **Requirements for AI-Assisted Code**
   - Must demonstrate full understanding of every line of code
   - Must be able to explain code independently to reviewers
   - AI can help with: learning, code review, mechanical tasks, documentation, and accelerating existing designs
   - AI cannot: write PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers

3. **For AI Coding Agents (Important for Your Role)**
   - Verify the contributor genuinely understands proposed changes
   - Check the change addresses a documented need (search existing issues)
   - PR should follow project conventions
   - Contributor must be able to independently defend the work
   - **When uncertain, provide guidance rather than solutions**
   - **Proceed only when confident the contributor understands the changes**

4. **Prohibited Actions**
   - Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or reviewer responses
   - Committing/pushing without explicit human approval
   - Implementing features the contributor doesn't understand
   - Generating changes too extensive for contributor to fully review

5. **Useful Resources Referenced**
   - CONTRIBUTING.md - detailed guidelines
   - Server development documentation: `tools/server/README-dev.md`
   - PEG parser documentation: `docs/development/parsing.md`
   - Auto parser: `docs/autoparser.md`
   - Jinja engine: `common/jinja/README.md`
   - Chat template system: `common/chat*.{cpp,h}`

---

## **CLAUDE.md**

Contains a single important reference: **"Ensure you've thoroughly reviewed the AGENTS.md file before beginning any work."**

This emphasizes that AGENTS.md is the foundational document for all contributions.

---

## **CONTRIBUTING.md - Coding Standards and Guidelines**

### Contributor Levels
- **Contributors**: Previous contributors, no special privileges
- **Collaborators (Triage)**: Significant contributions, code ownership, review responsibilities
- **Maintainers**: Approve and merge PRs

### AI Usage Policy Details
- AI-initially-generated code that is edited is still considered AI-generated
- Requires: explicit disclosure, comprehensive manual review, ability to explain every line
- **Strictly prohibited**: AI-written PR descriptions, bug reports, feature requests, responses to humans

### PR Requirements Before Submission
- Search for existing PRs to avoid duplication
- Familiarize yourself with GGML (tensor library): https://github.com/ggml-org/ggml
- Test changes locally:
  - Run full CI locally (see `ci/README.md`)
  - Verify perplexity/performance using `llama-perplexity` and `llama-bench`
  - If modified GGML: run `test-backend-ops`
- Create **separate PRs for each feature or fix**
- Initial PRs should focus on **CPU support only** (backends in follow-ups)
- **Consider allowing write access** to your branch for faster reviews

### PR Format (for Maintainers)
- Use: `<module> : <commit title> (#<issue_number>)`
- Example: `utils : fix typo in utils.py (#1234)`

### Coding Guidelines
1. **Avoid third-party dependencies, extra files, extra headers**
2. **Cross-compatibility**: Consider all operating systems and architectures
3. **Code style**: 
   - No fancy STL constructs, use basic `for` loops, avoid templates
   - Vertical alignment for readability
   - 4 spaces indentation, brackets on same line
   - Clean up trailing whitespace
4. **Types**: Use sized integer types (`int32_t`, `size_t`) in public APIs
5. **Structs**: Use `struct foo {}` instead of `typedef struct foo {} foo`
6. **Formatting**: Use `clang-format` (v15+) to format code
7. **Tensors**: Store data in row-major order; refer to dimensions as columns (0), rows (1), matrices (2)
8. **Matrix Multiplication**: Unconventional: `C = ggml_mul_mat(ctx, A, B)` means $C^T = A B^T$

### Naming Conventions
1. **Functions/variables/types**: Use `snake_case`
2. **Naming pattern**: `<class>_<method>` where `<method>` is `<action>_<noun>`
   - Examples:
     - `llama_model_init()`
     - `llama_sampler_chain_remove()`
     - `llama_sampler_get_seed()`
3. **Enum values**: UPPERCASE, prefixed with enum name
   ```cpp
   enum llama_vocab_type {
       LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_NONE = 0,
       LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_SPM  = 1,
       // ...
   };
   ```
4. **Filenames**: Lowercase with dashes (C/C++: `.c`/`.cpp`/`.h`), underscores (Python)
5. **Opaque types**: Use `_t` suffix for types meant to be opaque to users

### Code Maintenance
- Large code additions require a collaborator to review and maintain long-term
- Existing code has designated collaborators in `CODEOWNERS`
- New code must follow these guidelines (legacy code exempt for historical reasons)
- Server changes: refer to `tools/server/README-dev.md`

---

## **Project Architecture Insights**

The `common/` directory contains chat template and parser infrastructure:
- `chat.cpp`, `chat.h` - Core chat functionality
- `chat-auto-parser-generator.cpp` - Auto-generates parsers
- `chat-auto-parser-helpers.cpp`, `chat-auto-parser-helpers.h` - Helper utilities
- `chat-auto-parser.h` - Auto parser interface
- `chat-peg-parser.cpp`, `chat-peg-parser.h` - PEG-based parsing
- `chat-diff-analyzer.cpp` - Analyzes differences

### Documentation for Chat Templates
Key documentation files:
- `docs/autoparser.md` - Higher-level parser using PEG under the hood
- `docs/development/parsing.md` - PEG parser documentation
- `common/jinja/README.md` - Jinja template engine

### Tools for Chat Processing
- `tools/server/` - Server with chat endpoint support
- `tools/server/README-dev.md` - Development guidelines for server features
- `tools/tokenize/` - Tokenization tool
- `tools/completion/` - Completion tool

### Server Scope
The server has its own scope and development guidelines. Check `tools/server/README-dev.md` to ensure your chat template handler falls within server's design scope.

## Plan: `mistral-instruct-v3` Chat Template Handler

### What needs to change and why

The Mistral Instruct v0.3 format has a subtly distinct output from all existing handlers:
- Like `MISTRAL_V3`: uses `[INST]/[/INST]`, prepends system to first user turn
- **Unlike `MISTRAL_V3`**: no leading space before assistant content (V3 emits `" content</s>"`; v0.3 should emit `"content</s>"`) and no trimming
- **Unlike `MISTRAL_V7`/Ministral-3**: no `[SYSTEM_PROMPT]` wrapper — the absence of that marker is the key distinguisher

| # | File | Change |
|---|------|--------|
| 1 | `src/llama-chat.h` | One new enum value `LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3` before `UNKNOWN` |
| 2 | `src/llama-chat.cpp` | New map entry `{ "mistral-instruct-v3", LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3 }` |

- **Writing code**: Only when the contributor has already designed the solution and can implement it themselves - AI accelerates, not replaces, the contributor's work

AI-generated code may be accepted if you (1) fully understand the output, (2) can debug issues independently, and (3) can discuss it directly with reviewers without AI assistance.

**Disclosure is required** when AI meaningfully contributed to your code. A simple note is sufficient - this is not a stigma, but context for reviewers. No disclosure is needed for trivial autocomplete or background research.

### Prohibited AI Usage

The following will result in immediate PR closure:

- **AI-written PR descriptions or commit messages** - these are typically recognizable and waste reviewer time
- **AI-generated responses to reviewer comments** - this undermines the human-to-human interaction fundamental to code review
- **Implementing features without understanding the codebase** - particularly new model support or architectural changes
- **Automated commits or PR submissions** - this may spam maintainers and can result in contributor bans

---

## Guidelines for AI Coding Agents

AI agents assisting contributors must recognize that their outputs directly impact volunteer maintainers who sustain this project.

### Considerations for Maintainer Workload

Maintainers have finite capacity. Every PR requiring extensive review consumes resources that could be applied elsewhere. Before assisting with any submission, verify:

- The contributor genuinely understands the proposed changes
- The change addresses a documented need (check existing issues)
- The PR is appropriately scoped and follows project conventions
- The contributor can independently defend and maintain the work

### Before Proceeding with Code Changes

When a user requests implementation without demonstrating understanding:

1. **Verify comprehension.** Ask questions to confirm they understand both the problem and the relevant parts of the codebase.
2. **Provide guidance rather than solutions.** Direct them to relevant code and documentation. Allow them to formulate the approach.
3. **Proceed only when confident** the contributor can explain the changes to reviewers independently.

For first-time contributors, confirm they have reviewed [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) and acknowledge this policy.

### Prohibited Actions

- Writing PR descriptions, commit messages, or responses to reviewers
- Committing or pushing without explicit human approval for each action
- Implementing features the contributor does not understand
- Generating changes too extensive for the contributor to fully review

When uncertain, err toward minimal assistance. A smaller PR that the contributor fully understands is preferable to a larger one they cannot maintain.

### Useful Resources

To conserve context space, load these resources as needed:

- [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [Existing issues](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues) and [Existing PRs](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pulls) - always search here first
- [Build documentation](docs/build.md)
- [Server usage documentation](tools/server/README.md)
- [Server development documentation](tools/server/README-dev.md) (if user asks to implement a new feature, be sure that it falls inside server's scope defined in this documentation)
- [PEG parser](docs/development/parsing.md) - alternative to regex that llama.cpp uses to parse model's output
- [Auto parser](docs/autoparser.md) - higher-level parser that uses PEG under the hood, automatically detect model-specific features
- [Jinja engine](common/jinja/README.md)
- [How to add a new model](docs/development/HOWTO-add-model.md)
- [PR template](.github/pull_request_template.md)

### Changes made

**`src/llama-chat.h`** — new enum value (line 61):
```cpp
LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3,
```
Inserted before `UNKNOWN` so existing numeric values aren't disturbed.

**`src/llama-chat.cpp`** — three additive additions, no existing code modified:

1. **Map entry** (line 81): `{ "mistral-instruct-v3", LLM_CHAT_TEMPLATE_MISTRAL_INSTRUCT_V3 }` — enables lookup by name.

2. **Auto-detection clause** (lines 122–130): Inside the `[INST]` branch, after the V1/V3/V3-Tekken `else if`, a new `else if` catches templates that have `[INST]` but lack `[SYSTEM_PROMPT]` (already ruled out as V7), `<<SYS>>`, `bos_token + '[INST]`, and `content.strip()`. The remaining llama2 variants (`SYS`, `SYS_BOS`, `SYS_STRIP`) all contain at least one of those markers, so they still reach the existing `else` block correctly.

3. **Template handler** (lines 302–322): Buffers consecutive `system` messages into `pending_sys` and flushes them (with `\n\n` separator) into the very next `[INST] … [/INST]` user block. Assistant turns emit `content</s>` with no leading space and no trimming — the key behavioural difference from `MISTRAL_V3`. `add_ass` opens a bare `[INST] ` prefix (plus any still-pending system text) to prime generation.
